summary:
The Viceroyalty of Peru (1542–1824 AD) was the administrative unit through which Spain governed most of South America for nearly three centuries — centered on the capital Lima, founded in 1535 by Francisco Pizarro and deliberately established on the coast (rather than in the Andean highlands where the [[event:2320|Inca Empire]] had been centered) to facilitate communication with Spain by sea. At its greatest extent, the Viceroyalty encompassed all of Spanish South America, though subsequent administrative divisions created the viceroyalties of New Granada (1717) and Río de la Plata (1776), reducing Peru to its core territories of modern Peru and Bolivia.

Lima became one of the wealthiest and most sophisticated cities in the Spanish colonial world — a center of colonial administration, Catholic ecclesiastical authority (the headquarters of the Inquisition in South America), and commercial activity centered on the silver trade from [[event:2425|Potosí]] and other mines. The University of San Marcos (founded 1551) was the first university in the Americas. The viceroyalty's social structure was rigidly hierarchical: peninsulares (Spanish-born) at the top, creoles (American-born Spaniards) below them, followed by mestizos, free Indigenous people, and enslaved Africans, with the mass of the Indigenous population subject to the mita (forced labor) system. The massive [[event:2465|Túpac Amaru II rebellion]] (1780) was the most serious challenge to colonial order; the Viceroyalty survived it but emerged weakened, and Lima fell to independence forces under San Martín in 1821, ending nearly 300 years of Spanish rule.
